subject: This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI — Date of April 15, 2026
body:
This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI

Five papers worth your time at the intersection of real-world evidence, causal inference, and artificial intelligence.

Short Versus Longer Antibiotic Duration for Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Multicenter Target Trial Emulation

Doumat et al. — PubMed | Annals of Internal Medicine, April 2026

The story practitioners should take from this paper is not the underpowered mortality estimate — it is the 10.1% eligibility rate. Target trial emulation across 67 Michigan hospitals shows that the vast majority of hospitalized CAP patients do not meet the strict criteria for short-course antibiotic therapy, even when restricting to those who achieve clinical stability by day 3. The gap between guideline aspiration and real-world eligibility is the antibiotic stewardship problem, and this TTE at scale is the evidence that makes it undeniable.

Comparative Cardiovascular Effectiveness of GLP-1RAs versus DPP-4is in Dialysis Patients with Type 2 Diabetes: A Target Trial Emulation

Shimada et al. — PubMed | Diabetes & Metabolism, April 2026

Dialysis patients are the pharmacoepidemiology black hole of the GLP-1 era — every major CVOT excluded them, and a dedicated RCT is not coming. Shimada et al. use target trial emulation with time-varying IPW in a Japanese administrative claims database to estimate a risk difference of −8.0 percentage points for 3-year MACE with GLP-1RA versus DPP-4i (95% CI: −15.5 to 0.9). The confidence interval crossing null is a power problem, not a design problem — and this TTE is the only evidence base this population will ever get for this question. The per-protocol estimation with time-varying confounding adjustment is the template for filling cardiovascular RWE gaps in populations that RCTs will never enroll.

Using Causal Machine Learning and Real World Data to Improve Dose Response Decision Making for Secukinumab in Psoriatic Arthritis

Kiltz et al. — PubMed | Scientific Reports, April 2026

Read the conflict of interest disclosure first: five of six authors are from Novartis, the manufacturer of secukinumab. With that front and center, the methodology is instructive. Kiltz et al. apply double machine learning to 42 baseline variables from the real-world AQUILA study to estimate conditional average treatment effects for dose escalation in psoriatic arthritis. The finding that DML can stratify the ~41% of patients who actually benefit from dose escalation — including a 28% HR-QoL gain in high-BMI/high-CRP subgroups — demonstrates exactly how CATE estimation from RWD should be operationalized to personalize biologics dosing decisions.

A Case Study Comparing Anonymized and Synthetic Health Insurance Claims Data for Medication Safety Assessments

Halilovic et al. — PubMed | NPJ Digital Medicine, April 2026

As the European Health Data Space and analogous data-sharing initiatives push for privacy-preserving alternatives to raw data access, the choice between anonymization and synthesis needs an empirical foundation — and this paper provides one. Both approaches reproduce hazard ratios close to the original analysis, but both methods inflate statistical uncertainty, and higher data utility reliably comes with higher privacy risk — a trade-off with no technical resolution. Teams relying on synthetic or anonymized claims for regulatory submissions should quantify the uncertainty inflation, not just validate point estimates.

Evaluating Real-World Deployment of an HL7-CDA-Aligned LLM for ICD-10-CM Coding

Dai et al. — PubMed | NPJ Digital Medicine, April 2026

Most LLM-in-clinical-workflow papers test AI in sandboxed conditions. Dai et al. ran a 13-week randomized trial with ten certified coding specialists in a live hospital system: AI-assisted coding significantly reduced time-to-code, but satisfaction varied systematically by coder experience, certification level, and generational cohort. The lesson — model accuracy is a necessary but not sufficient condition for real-world deployment success — is precisely what model-benchmarking papers cannot teach you. The human factors data should be required reading for any team planning a clinical LLM rollout.

Browse the full reading list: blackswancausallabs.com/reading-list.html
